rows.title: Croatian DPA Fines Real Estate Agency EUR 100,000 for Storage, Lawfulness and Security Failures
rows.summary:
The Croatian Personal Data Protection Agency (AZOP) has imposed an administrative fine of EUR 100,000 on a real estate agency acting as a data controller for multiple infringements of the General Data Protection Regulation, including unlawful retention of client data, processing of identification documents without legal basis, and failure to implement adequate technical and organisational measures.

On 19 February 2026, the Agencija za zaštitu osobnih podataka issued its decision following a supervisory procedure during which AZOP identified three distinct GDPR violations. AZOP levied a fine of EUR 100,000 against the unnamed real estate agency in respect of infringements of Articles 5(1)(e), 5(1)(c), 6(1) and 32(4) GDPR.

AZOP finds that the controller retained personal data belonging to 11,887 clients beyond the period for which those data were required, in breach of the storage limitation principle (Art. 5(1)(e) GDPR). The agency's paper archive contained forms titled Mediation Agreement for the Purchase of Real Estate, Mediation Agreement for the Rental/Lease of Real Estate, and Broker's Sheet, concluded between 23 September 2010 and 31 December 2019 at the latest. AZOP further determines that the controller processed, without a demonstrated legal basis, 914 copies of personal identification and financial documents, including 898 identity card copies, 6 passport copies, 3 bank card copies, 2 driving licence copies, 2 foreign passport copies, and individual copies of a health insurance card, a minor child's identity card, and a residence permit, contrary to Article 6(1) in conjunction with Article 5(1)(c) GDPR. AZOP notes that during the inspection the company's director stated that bank card copies were not collected, yet such copies were found within the mediation documentation, which AZOP determines further confirms a lack of oversight over processing operations.

With regard to technical and organisational measures, AZOP establishes that the controller failed to ensure adequate training and supervision of employees with access to personal data, in breach of Article 32(4) GDPR. AZOP finds that data protection training was conducted irregularly and to an insufficient extent. AZOP concludes that the non-compliance resulted from the controller's negligent conduct and that no harm to data subjects was established in this specific case.
